data.title: Deere & Company: Outrunning Broader Markets, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Deere & Company (DE) has been one surprising winner over the first few months of the 2026 calendar year. With shares down 9% over the past month, I believe DE’s performance and outlook are worth a revisit.

DE has gained in part because of the rotation away from tech-related names that have been viewed as at risk of obsolescence from AI-related changes.

These names include software stocks like Adobe (ADBE), which is down over 30% YTD and trading at historically low levels. I’ve viewed these declines very opportunistically and have provided bullish coverage on many of these beaten-down software names.

Shares in DE, on the other hand, are enjoying the positive momentum from the rotation. The stock is up 20% over the past year 25% YTD, and shares are sitting at the upper end of its 52-week range.

Times are good, for sure. While I view the outlook ahead as positive, I believe investors could benefit from waiting for a more attractive entry point as opposed to chasing the current momentum.

DE Stock Key Metrics

Shares currently are trading at a 32x forward multiple. That’s a steep multiple, in my view.

The sector average is about 19x, and DE’s own historical average is around there as well. In addition, DE’s current price is nearly 4x sales. That’s about double the ratio for the sector average and well above DE’s range.

It’s not a surprise to me that DE grades poorly on valuation by Seeking Alpha’s (“SA”) quant scores.

Some, however, would argue that the premium is justified, especially given DE’s relative insulation to AI-based change, as well as its own leveraging of the tool to further its own business.

Though the stock is relatively undercovered by the broader SA community, those that have provided coverage have skewed bullish according to the listed metrics. I would say, however, that most of the recent coverage has been neutral.

The stock is more covered by the Wall Street community, and they are primarily bullish. However, consensus price targets see only modest upside potential remaining in the stock.

At the current consensus, DE is seen as fairly valued at around the $660/share mark.

That’s 14% higher than current levels. It’s a positive return, yes. But could better returns be achieved elsewhere? I certainly believe so.

DE Valuation

DE currently trades around 32x forward earnings and nearly 4x sales. That’s comparable to peer Caterpillar (CAT), but it’s higher than the broader sector average. Deere itself has historically traded much closer to the 20x levels through prior cycles.

Bulls may argue that the multiple is deserved. DE does, after all, carry higher margins, stronger brand equity, and tighter dealer/channel discipline and is far more advanced in precision ag and digital integration than peers.

The company is also demonstrating through-cycle cost control and balance sheet strength that many competitors struggle to replicate.

Still, even if DE hits the high end of its net income guide, I believe that today’s price implies that much of the cycle recovery, margin durability, and “AI-immune” narrative is already priced in.

Consensus targets suggest only mid-teens upside from here. Respectable, but not compelling relative to other opportunities in the market, in my view.

DE Stock Operating Performance

Some would say that DE’s Q1 results that were released in February showed why DE is valued as it is, with the topline growing double digits and ahead of expectations.

Drilling into the numbers, net sales for equipment operations grew 18% YOY, while total revenue rose 13%. This was ahead of expectations, driven primarily by stronger-than-expected shipment volumes across multiple segments.

One of the bigger stories of Q1 was the segment strength outside large agriculture (“ag”). Small Ag & Turf, for example, posted 24% sales growth with a 9% operating margin, while Construction & Forestry grew 34% with margins more than doubling YOY.

Both segments benefited from higher retail settlements, strengthening order books, and production efficiencies that more than offset tariff headwinds.

The latter segment is definitely becoming a bright spot for DE, and this is supported by infrastructure spending, rental demand, and data center development.

On the other side of the coin, large ag remains pressured. Still, conditions appear to be stabilizing. In Q1, order velocity for large tractors improved, used inventory levels continued to decline, and there was marked visibility into orders extending into the fourth quarter.

Another highlight of Q1 was cost discipline. Ex-tariffs, production costs were lower across all segments due to operational efficiencies and overhead control. This shows the management team's flexibility and skill in navigating a more challenged economic environment, in my view.

What Is The Outlook For DE Stock?

Looking ahead to DE’s full fiscal year, DE is expecting mid-single-digit net sales growth for equipment operations, with strength coming from Small Ag & Turf and Construction & Forestry, both of which saw their full-year sales outlooks raised by 5 points after Q1 came in ahead of plan. This makes sense, and I can see DE hitting this target.

Small Ag & Turf is now projected to grow sales about 15% with operating margins between 13.5% and 15%. Construction & Forestry carries a similar 15% growth outlook with margins expanding to 9%–11%.

Even Production & Precision Ag, which is still forecasted to decline 5%–10%, is expected to hold healthy double-digit margins as North American order activity improves and used inventories continue to normalize.

At the enterprise level, Deere lifted net income guidance and increased operating cash flow expectations by +$500M at both ends of the range.

Even more important than the numbers, in my view, is how well DE fits into what investors have recently labeled the “HALO” trade. Heavy assets, low obsolescence.

You cannot disrupt tractors, combines, excavators, and roadbuilding equipment with an AI prompt. In fact, DE is embedding AI and precision tech into its machines to make them more productive. This is basically furthering DE’s moat rather than threatening it.

I expect the competitive advantage of purely its business model to continue serving DE’s stock well through the remainder of 2026.

Is DE Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

In my view, DE is doing just about everything right operationally.

Shipments are ahead of plan. Order books are strengthening. Inventories are healthy. Cost discipline is there. Guidance was raised. And the management team clearly believes the company is moving through the bottom of the cycle with improving visibility into the back half of the year.

On top of that, Deere has found itself in a very favorable market narrative. In a year when investors are questioning which businesses are vulnerable to AI disruption, DE sits in the opposite camp.

Its machines are essential, asset-heavy, and increasingly enhanced by technology rather than threatened by it. I believe it’s clear that the “HALO” characteristic has absolutely contributed to the stock’s strong performance so far this year.

At roughly 32x forward earnings, however, and nearly double the sector’s price-to-sales ratio, I believe the stock already reflects a lot of this good news.

While consensus still sees modest upside, I believe investors can find more attractive opportunities elsewhere and revisit DE on a pullback. For long-term holders, I don’t see any reason to sell. But for me, DE is a clear ‘hold’.

data.title: AI Panic Gave Us An Altus Group Stock Bargain
data.description:
The recent AI-driven software selloff beat down some good stocks; Altus Group Limited (AIF:CA) is one of the best.

Altus owns Argus, a vertical software solution for commercial real estate focused on property valuation and asset management. The software operates a real estate-focused cash flow-engine model. It has been the industry standard for decades.

Argus has a network effect in which parties in CRE deals and valuation work need to operate from assumptions from the same database. That rent roll allows brokers, investors, appraisers, and lenders to work from the same source. Real estate analysts are trained on the platform, creating a common language for CRE. It is common for limited partnership agreements to require Argus for portfolio valuations. In short, it is the industry standard.

Shifting to the cloud further entrenched Argus by enabling it to host the databases of tenant data and lease terms on their own servers. By agreeing to contribute their data to this solution, customers can now access detailed benchmarking, transactional, and other data that they could otherwise never access with such precision and depth. This IP creates a wide moat. A quasi-monopoly, even.

Shares trade at over an 8% 2027 free cash flow yield. And this cash flow is growing over 15% per year. Net revenue retention is over 100%. The trite concern is that this business is ripe for AI disruption, but it is already priced for that. The company intends to buy back over 30% of outstanding shares around current prices.

Consistent with their intent to return capital, the company just announced a substantial issuer bid to repurchase up to C$200 million of shares via a modified Dutch auction. If fully executed, the offer would retire ~10-12% of current outstanding shares. Shares are available for ~C$47 and will be bought back for C$42-52. Based on the shareholder base, it is likely to get ~$52 for any shares tendered.

The tender is very likely to occur at the high-end of the Dutch auction range for a few reasons. Edgepoint and Jarislowsky Fraser both declined to participate in the tender, as disclosed in the circular. Further, Long Path was recently purchasing shares at this price, and with a director on the Board, they would need to disclose their participation in the tender, which they have not. This eliminates >30% of the stock from participating. Further, the majority of shares are held by U.S. and Canadian funds, which, due to disastrous tax treatment with a low paid-up capital balance for the Altus shares, means those funds are better off selling stock at 47 than tendering at 52. Hence, they will not participate in the tender.

As a result, there is very little float left to participate, and if the full C$200mm is not filled, the tender automatically goes off at the high-end of the range, C$52/share. To further support this, the Company previously did a tender in January and only achieved C$162mm participation despite being at a higher price at the high-end (C$57) and with the participation of Jarislowsky Fraser in that tender.

This market dynamic creates a structural advantage for individuals investing out of retirement accounts that are able to execute this trade in a tax-free manner and avoid the withholding tax that a U.S. or Cayman fund would be subject to.

This is not me giving financial advice, and it is 10x not me giving tax advice. Buyer, (always) beware. Do your own work. Think for yourself. But probably ignore this one if you’re Cayman domiciled (no tax treaty, so you could permanently lose withholding) or for a U.S. taxable account. But it could be interesting for American or Canadian tax-advantaged accounts if you are comfortable with the taxes and file the paperwork.

Tax complications make this somewhere between annoying and disastrous for taxpayers, but this could be an opportunity for tax-advantaged accounts or entities. Canadian pension funds should gobble these shares up for a ~C$5 gain. The bad tax implications for others are what’s keeping the share price down. Were it not for the tax issues, it would probably trade >$50 per share today.

AIF:CA could be a good allocation for a tax-advantaged account. You can buy the shares today and then tender them at the top of the range.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NVDW: Alternative Strategy For Investors Seeking Income Oriented Exposure To Nvidia
data.description:
The Roundhill NVDA WeeklyPay ETF (NVDW) is an actively managed, leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with weekly income with the target of achieving 120% of the weekly performance of Nvidia (NVDA) shares. NVDW has historically paid out an exceptionally high distribution rate, with 100% being derived from return of capital, which is relatively common for derivative income funds.

Appeal of NVDW

NVDW can provide investors with differentiated returns with respect to NVDA shares, earning ROC in exchange for share price returns, effectively making NVDW an income-oriented strategy. Another appeal of the strategy is that the underlying swap derivatives are settled at the end of each calendar week rather than on a daily basis, elongating the period for which returns are compounded. This may mean that NVDW can be held for longer than a single trading day without the risk of daily compounded returns as experienced in more traditional leveraged ETFs, though it may add pricing risk in relation to large movements in the underlying asset.

Despite the underlying risks, NVDW presents fluidity when compared to NVDA shares, presenting investors with a strategy that can be held for longer holding periods while earning returns on distributed capital.

Taking into consideration the overarching theme, I believe Nvidia presents a continued growth opportunity for 2026 given the continued growth pattern for AI data center development in the US and internationally. At a high level, the hyperscalers have a cumulative backlog of $1.63T, a compelling argument for the continued capital investment cycle that will drive an increased investment outlay for sourcing AI accelerators. While custom ASICs present competition to the market, the driving theme amongst the hyperscalers is the provision of optionality rather than a sole source for compute capacity, solidifying Nvidia’s position in the market for AI training and inferencing.

The challenge at hand is whether investor sentiment has waned towards the AI theme with the potential rotation out of growth and into value strategies. While this theme is prevalent when comparing the performance of the S&P 500 (SPY) to the growth (SPYG) and value (SPYV) indices, I don’t necessarily expect an exodus from growth stocks but rather, a reallocation of funds into other corners of the market. This may mean that NVDA shares’ performance may be somewhat flat for the time being until investors find direction for the mega-cap technology stock.

That being said, I’m long-term bullish on NVDA shares, which may be reflected in positive performance of NVDW for income-oriented investors. In terms of performance expectations, investors must weigh whether a deferred tax benefit is ideal for this investment profile or if capital growth is preferred. This will determine whether NVDW or NVDA shares are appropriate for one’s investment strategy.

About Roundhill NVDA WeeklyPay ETF

NVDW was launched on February 19, 2025 on the Cboe BZX Exchange. The strategy has an expense ratio of 99 bps, aligned with peer leveraged strategies. NVDW exhibits moderate liquidity with $108.45mm in net assets, with an average of $3.92mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis.

The strategy has paid out a robust weekly distribution rate annualized at $22.80/share over the last twelve months, yielding 65.95%. 100% of the distribution is derived from ROC, potentially resulting in NAV erosion over time and resulting in a share price decline over time.

When evaluating weekly pay funds, I believe investors should assess performance based both on total returns and price returns. Total returns involve the price return plus distributions, presenting a realistic perspective of performance when compared to the underlying NVDA shares.

Assessing price returns can provide investors with more insight into the costs associated with selling out of NVDW after accumulating ROC. In short, ROC will lower an investment’s cost basis with each distribution. Once the cost basis reaches $0/share, excess ROC will be taxed as capital gains. Given this factor, the declining share price can work to an investor’s benefit, as a lower selling price can narrow the total tax implication upon the sale of the investment, assuming the investment is held in a taxable account. If the investor utilizes a tax-efficient account, the tax-deferred benefit will be neglected while the capital gains tax impact will not be taxed.

NVDW employs weekly swap derivatives to gain 120% exposure to NVDA shares, potentially creating certain intricacies to the fund that should be considered. Rather than daily settlement, weekly swaps are settled at the end of the calendar week. This can be beneficial to investors to a certain extent, given that the compounding effect on the share price will be extended out to a week rather than a single trading day, making this product appropriate for longer holding periods when compared to traditional leveraged strategies. Given that the fund has an active market, investors can expect daily performance to be aligned with the 120% leverage employed on NVDA shares.

In addition to this, the weekly price settlement can potentially present an elongated expectation of the price performance of NVDA shares. What I mean by this is that rather than trading based on daily performance expectations, investors can trade NVDW based on their expectations of the calendar week’s price performance, potentially smoothing out daily volatility into a week. While this can be appealing, it may provide investors with mismatched performance with respect to the underlying shares and add greater risk to actual performance.

Risks Related to NVDW

NVDW employs weekly swap derivatives in order to gain 1.2x the weekly exposure to NVDA shares, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Weekly swap derivatives can be considered as a bet on the weekly price performance of the underlying shares, as the swap derivatives will be settled at the end of the calendar week rather than at the end of the trading day. This may result in uneven pricing between NVDW’s swaps and the price of NVDA shares. Distributions are derived from ROC and should be expected to reduce the net asset value of the fund over time. Investing in leveraged strategies for long holding periods may result in compounded returns that may skew performance beyond the intended 1.2x weekly multiple.

If actively trading NVDW, investors may be exposed to greater short-term capital gains taxes on top of trading fees, spread risk, and expenses. Investors should be mindful of liquidity when trading leveraged strategies, as tighter liquidity may result in wider spread risk, impacting the total return of the trade.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

NVDW can be an appealing alternative investment strategy for investors seeking leveraged exposure to NVDA shares while earning income in place of capital growth. While the fund may expose investors to the intricacies of typical leveraged funds, risks will be more moderate given that the swap derivatives are settled weekly rather than daily, potentially allowing for longer holding periods. Given my expected performance of NVDA shares, I am recommending NVDW with a Buy rating for income-focused investors.

data.title: Hope Wanes, USD Little Changed While Bonds And Stocks Weaken
data.description:
The US dollar (DXY) is trading in narrow trading ranges against the G10 currencies today, but the calm in the foreign exchange market belies stress in other parts of the capital markets. Equities and bonds have been sold. The hope that an end to the Middle East conflict has faltered amid the conflicting signals from the US, which is sending thousands of more troops to the region, fanning fears of a ground invasion of some time, while Tehran countered US proposals with a set of their own conditions. Front-month WTI and Brent oil futures are trading at new highs for the week.

President Trump’s initial 48-hour ultimatum was replaced with a five-day grace period, which ostensibly ends tomorrow. The uncertainty hangs heavy and can be expected to dampen risk appetites today and tomorrow barring constructive developments. The conflict can be on the edge of significant escalation or a resolution.

Prices

G10

• The euro was already struggling to sustain the upside momentum after stalling near $1.1630 yesterday in front of Monday’s high (~$1.1640), but the Iranian news agency rejection of the US ceasefire proposal was a buzzkill. The single currency subsequently was sold briefly through Tuesday’s low, slightly below $1.1560. It spent most of the North American afternoon trading in a narrow range below $1.1580. The euro is in a narrow range today, unable to make much headway above $1.1570, and hovering near a three-day low, slightly below $1.1550. A convincing break may target $1.1525 ahead of Monday’s low (~$1.1485). There are almost 2.6 bln euros of options at $1.15 and nearly 2 bln euros of options at $1.16 expiring today.

• Even without the benefit of higher US yields, the dollar crept higher against the Japanese yen yesterday. The session high was recorded in the NY afternoon near JPY159.50. This was marginally above Tuesday’s high (~JPY159.20) but below Monday’s high (~JPY159.65). The dollar remains firm but trading choppily between about JPY159.30 and JPY159.55. The market has not given up on JPY160.

• Sterling traded inside Tuesday’s range yesterday. And that range of about $1.3360-1.3435 was the narrowest range in two weeks. It has been pushed to a three-day low near $1.3335 today before recovering to about $1.3360. Nearby support may be seen around $1.3315-20, but the key is Monday’s wide range (~$1.3255-1.3480). Only a break of that range seems notable. Options for GBP1.3 bln at $1.3350 expire today.

• The Canadian dollar has come under pressure over this week. The greenback was initially sold to about CAD1.3670 on Monday, a five-day low, but recovered to settle around CAD1.3720. It reached CAD1.3785 on Tuesday, and yesterday tested CAD1.3820. It approached CAD1.3840 in Europe today. Initial resistance is seen around CAD1.3850, and above there, targets the CAD1.39 area. The US dollar settled above the 200-day moving average for the first time in two months. It also settled above the upper Bollinger Band (found ~CAD1.3830 today).

• The Australian dollar was turned back from a brief attempt on $0.7000 and was sold back to almost Tuesday’s low (~$0.6940). It slipped further today to a little below $0.6930. The Aussie remains within the range set Monday (~$0.6910-0.7060). Yet, for the third consecutive session, it frayed the lower Bollinger Band (~$0.6935 today). A break of $0.6900 is arguably more technically significant than a close above $0.7000. Options for almost A$1.25 bln at $0.6900 expire today.

EM

• The Mexican peso consolidated in uneventful activity yesterday near its best levels in the past week, apparently helped as it were by the risk-on mood that lifted stocks and bonds. Indeed, Mexico stocks and bonds were among the best performers yesterday. Its 10-year dollar bond yield fell 10 bp and the local currency bond yield fell almost 20 bp. The Bolsa rallied about 2.8%. The dollar has forged a floor around MXN17.67 in recent days. The 20-day moving average is near MXN17.67, and the greenback has not settled below it since the war began. The US dollar is trading higher today and approached MXN17.82. Tuesday’s high was slightly above MXN17.89, and a band of resistance may extend toward MXN17.94. It seems binary: Risk-on in response to a resolution and the peso, likely will return as a market favorite. Risk-off on escalation can push the dollar back above MXN18.00, where it topped out on an intraday basis twice this month.

• The dollar traded with a slightly firmer bias against the yuan yesterday and posted the highest settlement so far this week, slightly above CNH6.90. It is firmer today, near CNH6.9130 in the European morning. Monday’s high was closer to CNH6.92. The PBOC set the dollar’s reference rate at CNY6.9056. (CNY6.8911 yesterday). We had detected a subtle shift and had anticipated that last week could have been the first weekly increase in the dollar’s reference rate since last November. While this did not materialize, it could this week. Stay tuned.

• Indian markets were closed today for the Ram Navami holiday.

Other Markets

• Yesterday’s equity gains in North America failed to inspire buying today. The only Asia-Pacific market to resist the downdraft was New Zealand. South Korea led the retreat with a 3.2% drop, and the index of Chinese companies that trade in Hong Kong shed 2.25%. Europe’s Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is snapping a three-day advance and giving back most of yesterday’s 1.4% gain. US index (SPX) futures are off around 0.75-1.0%.

• Bonds are also being sold today. The 10-year JGB yield rose by two basis points, while European yields are mostly 6-9 bp higher. Italian bonds are the outlier, with the benchmark yield up 11 bp. The 10-year Treasury yield is up four basis points to a little above 4.37%.

• Gold and silver are trading heavily and are below yesterday’s lows. A break of $4400-10 could target $4350 for gold. A break of $67.50 could spur a move to $66 for silver.

• May WTI peaked yesterday slightly above $91.70. Today, it has not been below $90.70 and reached a three-day high a little above $94.00. A push above $95 leaves little in the way of a retest on $100. Monday’s high was near $101.65.

Data

• US weekly initial jobless claims may draw passing interest today. They have slipped lower in back-to-back weeks through March 13 for the first time this year. The March jobs report is out next Friday, April 3, and the early forecasts are coming in around 51k, after falling 92k in February, which was distorted by strikes and poor weather.

• Canada releases its establishment survey for January employment today. In December, when Canada reported an 8.2k increase in employment, the establishment survey reported a 35.4k decline. Canada reported a 24.8k decline in employment in January. This time series does not typically capture the market’s attention, and it seems particularly dated now.

• Mexico’s central bank meets today. Nearly half of the economists polled by Bloomberg expect a cut. The swaps market is pricing in steady policy. The mid-March CPI readings showed both the headline and core rates are above the upper end of the 2-4% target and may be accelerating. The economy does appear to have been struggling again before the war on Iran began.

• The eurozone’s M3 money supply grew 3.0% year-over-year in February after a 3.2% pace in January (initially 3.3%). Lending figures were little changed.

• As widely expected, Norway’s Norges Bank stood pat today, with its deposit rate at 4.0%. It was a hawkish hold in the sense that it signaled a hike would likely be appropriate, and the central bank’s projections showed a quarter-point hike before the end of the year. The swaps market had a hike fully discounted at the end of H1 '26 yesterday, and after today’s central bank meeting, it now has about a one-in-three chance of a 50 bp move. Before the war in Iran began, there was slightly more than a 10% chance of an increase by then.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nvidia: Improvements Made Me A Believer; I'm In (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to revisit and give an update on Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) as I dug deeper and discovered that it has become a lot more profitable on a fundamental basis and does not trade at a ridiculous valuation. This is quite a different story from the last time I covered it back exactly two years ago, when I said that the expectations were too high, yet I was proven wrong, and the stock skyrocketed another 86% vs. SPY’s (SPY) 26%.

My stance on NVDA has changed, given the massive demand for AI data centers, making me break my diversification rule over the last few months.

Briefly on Performance

Now that the company has released its full-year results, let’s have a look at how the revenues progressed. We can see the explosiveness continued throughout the year, unsurprisingly. The company cannot keep up with the ridiculous amount of demand for its chips due to the AI data center craze and the AI race by the five or six hyperscalers out there that continue to put up the money.

In the latest report, NVDA’s data center revenues still grew a whopping 67% to $193.5B from last year due to the accelerated demand. Graphics cards grew by over 57% as well, to $22.4B. NVDA continues to impress on the top-line performance, and it seems like it is not slowing down one bit.

On the profitability end of things, for the full year, NVDA’s margins did not take a massive hit. Gross margins did decrease around 400bps to 71%, but that is still an amazing result considering the pace at which its top line continues to grow. In Q4, the company’s gross margins actually improved to 75%, allowing the company to expand both at scale and profitability. It’s important to look at the unit economics. You would think that the company “sacrificed” 400bps in margins to get sales, but that’s not the case. NVDA sold far more units at still-premium economics. The prices actually increased aggressively in the last year, and the sales still continued to accelerate. As we can see in the graph below, margins are beginning to turn back up due to the strong Q4 performance.

Looking at other important efficiency and profitability metrics, we can see that the company is losing some efficiency in how its assets and shareholder capital have been used. However, these are still rather remarkable returns.

Additionally, the company boasts an astounding return on total capital, on par with other greats like Apple (AAPL), and much higher than the rest of the hyperscalers.

These are the returns an investor is willing to pay a premium for to own them. In my case, I am going to lower my margin of safety in my valuation model.

Going over to NVDA’s financial position, NVDA finished the year with around $10.6B in cash and equivalents, as well as $52B in marketable securities against around $8B in debt. This is pocket change for a company of this size, so of course, the financial position is remarkable. Any bank would be willing to lend it money without even thinking that the company is not going to be able to pay them back. And of course, NVDA is a cash cow. Over the last three years, the company’s cash from operations increased substantially, going from 29B in Jan ’24 to $102B by Jan ’26. And it doesn’t even spend a lot of capex, so all of this cash turns into free cash flow. Its asset-light model allows for a lot of scalability, as we have witnessed already. It barely spent $6B on capex. Compare that to the hyperscalers who are planning to spend over $100B each on AI data capex.

Overall, it is amazing how well the company has been performing for such a long time now. Since revenues exploded back in FY23, the momentum may have slowed a little, but it is not what anyone expected, especially me. The hyperscaler demand for NVDA’s products continues to amaze me and prove me wrong. Right now, the AI data center business is the only one that has been this lucrative in a long time, and NVDA will continue to grow at this pace for a few more years, I would say. It’s surprising to see that its share price hasn’t done much this past year and has been pretty much stalling since November of 2025. Some investors are getting antsy, and I think something is brewing.

The profitability narrative

Instead of focusing on the upcoming products that have been covered so many times in the past, I would like to focus on what is important to me right now, and that is the company’s fundamental picture. We all know that the next iterations of its data center products are selling extremely well. You cannot get ahold of it unless you already have a contract with the company, and even then, there are massive lead times due to capacity constraints.

This is the key point. The scarcity of capacity. We all saw what happened to the memory side of AI. In the past, I recommended strong buys for Micron (MU), Western Digital (WDC), and SanDisk (SDK). I don’t think I expected them to perform this well because, as I said earlier, I didn’t expect this issue to crop up. The issue of constrained capacity due to the AI race by the hyperscalers that are taking away all the products from everyone else to feed their AI dreams.

So, extrapolating from these comments, we can see three reasons why I believe NVDA will be able to maintain such profitability going forward. Due to the product scarcity, NVDA has a lot of pricing power. Jensen Huang doesn’t need explosive unit growth forever to preserve very high profitability. That is most likely going to come naturally, as every enterprise requires the best-in-class product for their AI ambitions, and you know for a fact that the hyperscalers have deep pockets and will not all of a sudden become greedy and refuse to buy when others will. That will cause the competition to get ahead, and there is nothing worse than that in their mind (in my opinion).

The scarcity remains the topic of the AI race, with advanced supply chains remaining a bottleneck even as Blackwell ramps and NVDA prepare for Rubin. All the new iterations in the future will most certainly come at a much higher margin profile, so NVDA will end up skewing the mix towards the next-gen high-end systems and rack-scale deployments rather than lower-value products. This way, it will maintain premium pricing.

Secondly, which plays into the whole premium pricing point, the architectural upgrades of the next product are marketed as a massive leap in terms of efficiency and performance per dollar compared to the prior generations. Of course, this is what the hyperscalers are going to eat up and will continue to pay premium prices for the next iterations of the chips. Whatever wins them the AI race, if it is at all winnable. NVDA is going to continue to iterate new versions of its platform and product every few years at higher prices and keep the ball rolling forever, essentially. These products do not have a lifetime guarantee and will be in need of replacement probably every three or so years. After Rubin, there are talks of the Feynman AI accelerator (named after physicist Richard Feynman) and Rosa CPU (named after physicist Rosalind Franklin) in 2028, making "Rosa Feynman" the name of the following generation of NVDA products.

Lastly, if the demand continues to be ahead of supply, NVDA can keep selling out everything it makes, which means it can maintain a favorable pricing mix forever. Forever is an exaggeration, but you get my point, I hope. Maintaining such a favorable pricing mix means margins will either remain at these already high levels or even increase further because of the mentioned scarcity in capacity.

So, as long as these three things remain in place—the scarcity, repeated performance jumps, and the insatiable hunger from the hyperscalers for more power—NVDA will not only remain extremely profitable but will continue to see amazing revenue growth that will trickle down straight into the bottom line. Huang sees NVDA’s accumulated sales reaching $1T through 2027. That is an astounding number, which is fully supported in my opinion and not at all a pipedream. Even though it may sound sarcastic.

Risks to consider

Being over a $4T company right now brings a lot of investors like me to ask, “How high can it keep climbing?” And time and time again, we are proven wrong. Looking at the past fundamentals, the company was just always too expensive. So, the risk is, yet again, what do the investors think is going to happen? Can the company keep the momentum going to double again over the next couple of years and reach over $8T in valuation? What happens if it misses once or twice? That dream will come crashing down. And bring the whole market down with it.

The normalization of capacity is also a talking point for the skeptics, saying that whenever all of these enablers of hyperscalers’ AI dreams, like TSMC (TSM), Micron (MU), and others, finally build out the capacity, NVDA will lose pricing power, and with that, margins. That is a valid concern, but you have to see that capacity is not going to come online this year. At the latest, maybe by the second half of 2027, with most seeing capacity constraints easing in 2028. I don’t think many think about what if the new capacity is still not going to be enough. What if the hyperscalers continue to demand more and more from NVDA and the mentioned semiconductor companies so that there is still not enough for everyone? Further buildouts will be required.

Speaking of hyperscalers, this is my main concern and the reason I have been trying to diversify my AI exposure for a while now. So, we know that the six biggest companies right now have all massively increased their AI capex spending for 2026. If I were to take a quick guess, these companies will be spending anywhere from $600B to $750B in AI capex in 2026, and that is not counting all other companies that are also increasing their capex. So, what happens if they (hyperscalers) decide that the return on investment is not what they expected? They are quick to do something rash, like firing thousands of people on a whim, like back in the COVID era, so I can fully see them shutting off the money tap if they deem enough is enough.

Valuation

It has been a while since I did a valuation model for NVDA, so let’s see an update. The company has become a lot more profitable since then, so I am expecting a very different picture.

For revenues, I see that analysts expect to see growth at around 72% for the next year, which is crazy, and it will drop down to around 30%. I’m going to use these two as well and taper off over the next decade to keep it conservative.

For margins, I am expecting the company to at least maintain and maybe slightly fluctuate over the next while. I believe that is very much achievable. At this rate, the company will go down to a single-digit P/E ratio.

The company’s WACC was a little too high for my liking. It was around 13.5%, which would be fine if I were looking at a very risky small cap, but we saw from the company’s fundamentals that this is a very strong and profitable company. So, I went with 11.5% as my discount rate, and with that said, NVDA’s fair value is around $270.

Closing Comments

This feels to me like when I revisited Micron and saw a fundamentally different company. NVDA is a lot more profitable, and right now trades at a much lower multiple due to the explosive AI growth story that made NVDA so much more profitable. I didn’t like the company’s profitability back then, but I am liking it right now, which makes me break my diversification rule slightly. I’ve said many times over the last few months that I am trying to get away from the whole AI narrative, but when such an opportunity presents itself, I must take it. Therefore, I am upgrading my rating to a buy and will be initiating a position shortly.

I can see the party lasting until at least 2028, and I know the risks I take on, but I am confident that NVDA will continue to grow as it has in the past. I may have missed the big money, but there is still money to be made.

data.title: Halliburton: Higher Oil, Higher Estimates Likely To Come
data.description:
The Energy sector is the lone positive S&P 500 (SP500) niche so far this month. Rising oil prices amid the war in Iran have kept WTI (CL1:COM) near $90 and Brent (CO1:COM) above $100 much of the time. That’s bad news for consumers, but great news for oil drillers and energy service names. One of them is Halliburton Company (HAL).

I had a Buy rating on HAL in January of 2025. Shares are up 39% since then, outperforming the S&P 500 by about 30 percentage points. Today, I continue liking what I see both fundamentally and technically. I am sticking with a Buy rating, but am lowering my price target and offering a fresh look at the chart.

HAL +54% YoY, Outperforming U.S. & ex-U.S. Stocks

Back in January, HAL reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $0.69 topped the Wall Street consensus expectation of $0.55, while revenue of $5.7 billion, up 1% from the same period last year, was a material $250 million beat. Its North American net sales fell, but double-digit revenue gains from Europe & Africa helped the stock the following session.

Indeed, HAL lifted 4.1% the next day, marking the third consecutive post-earnings pop. Looking ahead to the April 21 Q1 report, the options market prices in a somewhat high 5.2% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the report. The next ex-dividend date is slated for June 4, while short interest on the now $32 billion market cap Energy sector stock is notable at 4.8%. HAL yields 1.76% on a forward basis.

Looking back on the quarter that was, HAL delivered robust Q4 performance that beat estimates, driven primarily by healthy revenue and margins in its Completion and Production segment, better-than-feared seasonal fall-offs in North America, and high-margin completion tool sales in international markets. Over the October to December period, free cash flow of $875 million ($828 million ex asset sales) also topped expectations, aided by a working capital tailwind.

HAL: Operational Update

In terms of guidance, the management team sees the North American market down high single digits YoY. Elsewhere, the international business is expected to be flat to up modestly. In the upcoming report, I expect those numbers to potentially get ratcheted higher, given much stronger oil & gas prices today. And it will be interesting to see their comments and projections regarding Venezuela.

From a shareholder perspective, the firm remains focused on capital efficiency and free cash flow generation. It targets capex of 5% to 6% of revenue over the years ahead, which should preserve both the FCF yield and dividend yield. HAL maintains a strict shareholder return framework, too, committing to return at least 50% of its annual FC to shareholders through dividends and ongoing share repurchases. Last year, it returned 85% of free cash flow to investors.

HAL: High Shareholder Returns

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be slightly negative in FY 2026, but then rebound significantly in the out year and through FY 2028. All the while, revenue is seen as somewhat stable in the low to eventually mid-$20 billion range. Once again, I expect the street to begin lifting its top- and bottom-line HAL estimates, particularly once the Q1 report is in hand by the end of next month.

As it stands, there have been an even mix of sell side EPS upgrades and downgrades over the past 90 days. Finally, profitability trends are stout, with $1.96 of FCF per share over the past 12 months.

HAL: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates to my previous analysis, some 14 months ago. If we assume a normalized EPS of $3 and apply a 16x multiple, then shares should trade near $51. That is down from my prior $58 target, due to reduced EPS forecasts and a lower P/E.

I assert, however, that even $80-plus oil should help lift profits above current forecasts. What’s more, the mid-teens longer-term EPS growth rate should warrant at least a mid-teens multiple.

HAL: Near Trough Earnings, Low P/S Ratio

Key risks for HAL include weaker global oil and gas prices, along with the threat of a worldwide recession this year amid high inflation and rising interest rates. For the company specifically, I’d like to see improved North American operations and inroads to profitability from technological solutions—an area the firm specializes in.

Executives must also balance traditional oilfield services opportunities with power projects—there could be high-ROI initiatives slightly outside HAL’s core business.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q1 2026 earnings date of Tuesday, April 21 BMO. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With a more measured valuation and an unstable global oil landscape, HAL’s technical situation is strong. Notice in the chart below that shares have soared more than 100% from the April 2025 low. I don’t see resistance until the stock nears its 2022-2023 highs in the $43 to $45 range.

So, there’s still another 10%+ of upside, in my view. What’s more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is in a bullish zone, between 40 and 80, while the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also take a look at the ideal bullish rounded bottom formation that was put in last year. Though HAL plunged in the three months after my Q1 2025 assessment, the bulls quickly grabbed the reins. There is a downside gap, however, at $30 that could get filled if we see oil prices retreat back to, say, $70 on WTI.

But the recent thrust appears durable—it more than negated a potentially bearish RSI divergence (red on the chart below).

HAL: $44 Resistance, $30 Support, Rising 200dma, Strong RSI

The Bottom Line

I have a Buy rating on HAL. I expect a strong Q1 report and upbeat guidance, prompting a slew of Street upgrades at the end of next month. The valuation remains attractive for this Oil and Gas Equipment and Services company’s equity, while the chart points to a test of long-term resistance in the mid-$40s.

data.title: Why I Think The iHeartMedia 2027 Senior Unsecured Notes Are Attractively Priced
data.description:
Thesis

An opportunity to purchase the 8.375% 2027 senior unsecured high-yield notes of iHeartMedia (IHRT) exists for a sizeable yield to maturity & yield to worst of 18%. The company currently finds itself in a transition from legacy multiplatform audio to digital audio & podcasting. Given the $270 million cash on hand, $500 ABL facility, and the temporal seniority enjoyed by the notes coupled with cross-default protection, it is highly likely that the 2027 notes will pay out without any issues. Furthermore, the company is growing into its cash flows, and fears over non-payment of the notes may be misplaced. The notes may also be mispriced, currently sitting at a spread of over 1,300 bps over the 10Y treasuries owing to illiquidity and its de minimis size of the original issue and total debt outstanding for iHeartMedia. Given the transition, downside protection and safety of returns appear guaranteed by the notes as compared to the volatile equity of the company.

Executive Summary

The 2027 notes are callable and can be put back to the company in case of a "101 Change of Control" event or any covenant violation. The notes were issued by iHeart Communications in 2019, when it was restructuring after coming out of bankruptcy in 2018. Given that COVID-19 accelerated the transformation of many businesses, iHeart is currently transitioning from being a multiplatform legacy radio broadcaster to digital broadcasting and analytics.

Although the multiplatform revenue has been declining at an average of 4.5%, the digital audio segment has been growing at a clip, with an average 25% growth, and also boasts an ROA of over 60% compared to the 10% average of the legacy broadcasting segment. The digital audio segment currently makes up 34.30% of the revenue mix, with legacy broadcasting at 58.6% and media services at 7.03%. The fixed charge coverage ratio currently sits at 1.37x and the total debt to EBITDA at 10.46x. Although on the extreme ends of the spectrum, the company is likely to be able to generate enough free cash flows to be able to service the upcoming debt and interest.

At a time when the markets are broadly focusing on the "AI Trade" and CapEx, this is an opportunity to pick up a mispriced high-yield instrument for a sizeable target return. Further, although the markets express worry about the declining revenue of the multiplatform legacy broadcasting, the digital audio segment is more than making up for the decline.

Company Background

iHeartMedia is one of the leading audio companies in the U.S. based on their operating segment and on consumer reach. The company, which has over 860 broadcasting stations in over 160 markets and can be heard on over 500+ platforms, was founded in 1972. The company, then known as Clear Channel Communications, re-entered the public markets in 2014 after rebranding as iHeartMedia, which is the 'Holdco' for iHeartCommunications. The company entered Chapter 11 bankruptcy proceedings in 2018, after which it faced the COVID-19 pandemic.

The company primarily operates in the U.S. and has three reporting segments. Multiplatform Group, Digital Audio Group, and Media Services Group. The company is currently in a transition phase wherein it is growing its reach and focus via the Digital Audio group, which includes podcasting, digital services, and advertising. COVID-19 has also accelerated the progress of the digital audio group, and it now makes up almost 34.30% of the total revenue.

Given that the company operates on a cyclical advertising model and is also transitioning, the equity and credit markets are currently worried about the company's ability to repay its outstanding debt. Accordingly, the equity of the company has also been beaten down post COVID-19 and trades at a low PE of 4.07x. Sirius XM (SIRI), which is similar but operates on a subscription-based model, is listed as one of the chief competitors of the company.

Financial Metrics & Debt Stack

The company has $4.8B of debt currently outstanding. The largest payments are due in 2029 and 2030, with $2.7B and $1.3B coming due, respectively. Other than the 2029 TL, which requires a $5.4 million amortization every quarter, the company is not required to amortize its outstanding debt. In line with the rest of the industry, the company enjoys gross margins of 58% and net margins of 2.55%. The Digital Media group is growing rapidly and now makes up 34.3% of the total topline of the company. Further, in addition to PPE, the company also has roughly $600 million of FCC licenses on its books. Adjusting for non-cash items and one-time expenses, iHeartMedia has generated a profit every year other than 2020.

Given the concerns over its cyclical and non-sticky advertising business in tandem with its transition to digital audio, the company's equity has generally traded from between 2.33x to 4.07x PE ratio. Enjoying a fixed charge coverage ratio of 1.37x, iHeartMedia generated $0.63 EPS in FY2025.

The senior unsecured 2027 notes, of which only $72 million of the original $1.4 billion is outstanding, are due on May 1 next year. Given the strong liquidity position of the company, with $270 million cash on hand, $500 ABL, it should not be a problem to repay the notes. Additionally, as mentioned above, given the temporal seniority as well as the small and illiquid size of the notes, the markets have provided an opportunity to capture a large spread on a relatively low-risk instrument.

Why The Notes Are Trading Wide?

iHeartMedia's legacy 8.375% 2027 unsecured notes (17% YTM) trade ~1,000 bps wider than Sirius XM's senior notes (Ba3/BB+, 5.9% YTM on new 2032s) despite both issuers having bullet maturities and positive FCF trajectories. The differential reflects Sirius XM's recurring subscription model and vastly superior ratings vs. iHeart's ad-cyclical profile, 6.6x leverage, and CCC+ rating. That iHeartMedia recently exited bankruptcy, operates on an advertisement-based model, and also doesn't enjoy a "Buffett Bump" is also reflected not just in the notes that trade, but also in the beaten-down equity of the company. Furthermore, unlike its competitors, iHeartMedia's FCF is still recovering and stabilizing along with its EBITDA. That the majority of the $4.8B stack is due for iHeart commencing in 2029 is also a company-wide risk that the markets are pricing in.

However, while iHeartMedia's 2027 tranche has near-certain full payment/recovery due to de minimis size and cross-default mechanics, its pricing embeds broader issuer risk, which supports my "Buy" recommendation at current levels. That the company-wide risks as far as the debt wall and the macro transition of the industry do not apply to the 2027 legacy notes, adds to the attractiveness of the issue. Further, the illiquidity and the smaller size of the issue should prove to be a benefit for smaller and more nimble operators given the pricing inefficiencies.

Thesis Risks

Company-wide risks remain with the yet-to-stabilize FCF, huge debt stack that comes due in 2029 and 2030, as well as the ongoing transition in place. As iHeart has not hedged its interest payments, another consideration that must be factored in is the current interest rate regime and whether the rates might be pushed back up higher. While there should be minimal risk to the 2027 legacy notes, these risks should be factored in.

Given the strong liquidity position and operating parameters of the business as far as the senior unsecured notes are concerned, one may assume the risks for non-payment to be on the lower scale.

Conclusion

I would therefore conclude by mentioning that I think that an investor could buy the 2027 unsecured notes, which pay out an 8.375% coupon and yield 18%. Given the mispricing, attractive debt wall, minimal amortization, and more than adequate liquidity and potential asset sales, if required, this tranche provides a risk-adjusted compensation that makes this a compelling investment.

data.title: Hermès: Don't Miss Buying This Dip (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
When I last wrote about the French luxury fashion stock Hermès (OTCPK:HESAY) (OTCPK:HESAF) eight months ago, an 11-12% price correction looked possible. In actual fact, the stock has fallen by 23%. Has HESAY overcorrected? Let's find out.

Why is the Hermès stock falling?

A look at HESAY's price chart over the past year reveals that from the time I wrote in July 2025 until almost the end of January 2026, the stock was essentially trading flat. And then it nosedived, coinciding with LVMH's (OTCPK:LVMUY) disappointing full-year 2025 results. LVMH could well be called the pulse of the luxury fashion and accessories sector, given its size and its diverse products. So it's little wonder that continued weakness in the company casts a shadow on the rest of the luxury set as well.

While HESAY recovered from the LVMH impact by mid-February, its own results didn't bolster investor confidence. The results weren't bad, to be sure. But they don't bolster confidence for 2026 either. In its outlook, the company mentions economic and geopolitical uncertainties twice. Also, HESAY's forward non-GAAP P/E had touched over 50x at that time, indicating fair valuation for the stock.

The start of the US-Iran war saw another correction in HESAY. Between February 27 and March 3, the stock lost ~8% of its value, in line with the pullback in the stock markets as such. Consumer discretionary stocks have been particularly hard hit, with the State Street Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Income ETF (XLY) down by 5.4% in the past month, further explaining the pullback in HESAY.

3 reasons HESAY can rise now

From the end of January until now, the stock is down by 25%. This is only a good sign, though. There are now more reasons for an uptick than not. Here are three of them.

#1. Healthy revenue growth with an Asian revival

For the full year 2025, Hermès's revenues grew by 8.9% at constant exchange rates. Sure, they softened from the 14.7% increase seen in 2024, but considering the soft luxury market environment, the number is still rather healthy. Moreover, in H2 2025, revenue growth actually accelerated to 9.6% YoY compared with 8.1% YoY in H1 2025.

The regional breakup of revenues is also encouraging for two reasons. First, growth is spread across all regions, with Europe and the Americas managing double-digit percentage increases. Second, the Asia market also saw a 7% increase. The biggest driver continued to be Japan, but Asia-Pacific ex-Japan also saw a decent 5% rise.

In fact, its rise accelerated from 3% YoY in H1 2025. This is important since Asia-Pacific ex-Japan is the company's biggest regional market, with a 42% contribution to revenue in 2025. Presumably, the big Chinese luxury market is a significant contributor to the region's demand. And the acceleration in sales growth in the region indicates the China market could be coming back to life. This in turn could hold the company in good stead this year.

#2. High margins at a time of inflation risk

Recurring operating income, the company's preferred non-GAAP earnings measure, did well too. It rose by 6.8% at current exchange rates, and the margin inched up a shade to 41% (2024: 40.5%) as well.

A strong margin is particularly positive at a time of inflationary risks. The US-Iran war driven oil price spike is already showing up at the consumer level. The Fed also raised its PCE inflation expectations for 2026 in its updated economic projections for the US earlier this month. It now expects inflation at 2.7% (earlier: 2.4%).

The forecasts still indicate that inflation will remain broadly in check. But with the war dragging on and crude oil prices firmly staying above USD 100/bbl for the past two weeks, the fate of inflation hangs in balance. Segments like luxury, with already high margins, have an edge, in that they can absorb higher costs or pass them on to customers with limited impact on demand.

But the current scenario does indicate risks, especially considering that the company's net income attributable to owners, dropped by 1.7% in 2025 due to higher income taxes. While the extent of decline slowed down from 5.2% YoY in H1 2025, at EUR 4.52 billion, the figure was still around 1% lower than my forecast of EUR 4.55 billion. The net income margin at 28.3% also fell (2024: 30.3%), but remains strong nevertheless.

#3. Exceptionally attractive market multiples

With the recent weakness in net income, I assume that the earnings figure will remain flat in 2026. This results in a forward non-GAAP P/E of 38.2x, which is exceptionally low for HESAY. The stock's five-year average forward ratio is at 49x, indicating 25-30% upside to the stock right now. Even if earnings were assumed to decline by 5% this year, it would still have a 20% upside.

Macro risks are rising

The only big risk to HESAY is the unpredictable geopolitical and macro environment right now. If the stock markets continue to sag on account of continued uncertainties, the stock might be unable to rise meaningfully despite being undervalued from a historical standpoint. Also, if geopolitics have a dire impact on the macroeconomy, the luxury market could well land in a bigger funk.

While Hermès has held up admirably in a soft luxury environment, it's not entirely immune to slowing down in demand. Its sales growth has softened in the past year and could continue to do so this year too. This, in turn, can impact the extent of upside to the stock.

What's next?

For now, though, the impact on the macroeconomy is expected to be limited. This in turn suggests that the stock market may well stabilise soon enough, which could bring investors attention to stocks like HESAY. With its strong revenue growth, robust margins, and now also attractive market multiples, the latest price dip isn't one to miss. Even if it takes time to gather momentum again, it can still make for a fine investment over the longer term. I'm upgrading HESAY to Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Financial Modeling Of IonQ - A Quantum Leader Going Through Hypergrowth
data.description:
IonQ, Inc (IONQ) reported 2025 earnings last month, and the results came in eerily close to the projections in my previous article written in September of last year.

In that article, I pieced together all known acquisitions made by IonQ at the time, along with a rough estimate of organic growth, to come to a best guess of their 2025 and 2026 revenues.

In that same article, I also gave a brief background on the quantum industry as a whole, the strengths and weaknesses of the various quantum computing modalities, adjacent sectors such as quantum networking and sensing, and why I believe IonQ holds a pole position as the only full-stack quantum company providing end-to-end quantum solutions.

My view of IonQ's technological and market positions has not changed. With that in mind, I will focus solely on the financial aspects of the company in this update.

Review of 2025 Results

Back in September of last year, I estimated that IonQ would do $133M of revenue in 2025, excluding interest on their cash balance which I estimated to be $20M. The actual results came in very close at $130M revenue, although if we include actual cash interest of $56M, the total would be $185M which greatly trounced my estimate of $153M.

I under-estimated their interest on cash because of an additional capital raise in October that increased IonQ's total cash hoard to a gigantic $3.5B.

For 2026, I projected $267 of revenue (again excluding interest on cash) in the September article. IonQ's 2026 guidance came in at $225-245 last month. But given IonQ has a history of sandbagging and then beating the top-end of guidance, I am quite confident that they will meet or beat my previous estimate.

My Sep Estimate

Official Results
2025 Rev Actual ($M) 133 130
2025 Rev + Int Actual ($M) 153 185
2026 Rev Guidance ($M) 267 225-245

Let's talk a bit about organic growth. IonQ's CFO Inder Singh said this on the conference call:

Importantly, our 2025 results included nearly 80% year-on-year organic growth, and I expect this to be even healthier in 2026.

IonQ's 2024 revenue was only $43M. 80% organic growth would put it at $77M - a bit more than the $65M I estimated in my old article, which means I may have overestimated the acquired components of IonQ's revenue by $12M - not surprising as the number I got was patched together from multiple private market sources. Had IonQ only achieved organic revenue of $65M, that would still amount to organic growth of 51%.

Anyway you look at it, this pours cold water on the typical bear thesis that all of IonQ's growth was acquired and they have "no organic growth".

This company is too early in its hypergrowth phase for us to look at profitability. However, we should consider its cash burn to determine how much of a runway they have before they need to become profitable.

In 2025, IonQ used $16.4M of cash on CapEx and $283.2M on operating activities, for a total cash burn of $299.6M. Given the significant business changes and combinations that happened throughout the year, it's probably a good idea to sanity check this number with the Q4 cash burn. IonQ does not provide quarter-by-quarter cashflow, but if we use Q4 Adjusted EBITDA of -67.4M as proxy, their annualized cash burn would be $270M. To be conservative, I will use the larger number of $299.6M/year as their cash burn rate.

This gives them more than 11 years of runway based on year-end liquidity position of $3.3B.

Near-term Projection (2026/2027)

We have quite clear visibility into how IonQ will do in 2026 and even in 2027.

IonQ is expected to close 3 acquisitions this year. One is Skyloom, a developer of lightwave-optics communications technology that will enable satellite-to-satellite quantum networks. This was completed as of Jan 28.

Another deal was Seed Innovations - a quantum software engineering firm which closed on Jan 30.

IonQ issued about 5M shares for these 2 acquisitions which is roughly 1.4% share dilution. According to ZoomInfo, Skyloom's annual revenue was approximately $22M, and Seed Innovations was doing <$5M in revenue.

Both of these acquisitions closed in January and IonQ's earnings release was in February, so we have to assume that their 2026 guidance already incorporated revenues from these 2 acquisitions.

The 3rd and most significant deal is SkyWater (SKYT), a US-based semiconductor foundry with exposure to manufacturing quantum circuits. This deal turns IonQ into the world's first fully vertically integrated quantum platform company and greatly accelerates their quantum computing roadmap. It is widely expected to close by Q2 or Q3 of this year.

Let's first estimate where revenue will be without SkyWater.

In terms of organic growth, CFO's words quoted in the previous section indicated that 2026 will be "healthier" than 2025's 80% growth.

Conservatively assuming just 80% organic growth (i.e. disregarding IonQ's habit of lowballing) on 2025 revenue of $130M (rather than on 2025 annual run-rate inclusive of full year results of all the companies they bought in the year, which would give an even higher baseline of $200M+) gives us $234M in 2026.

Adding 11 months of revenue from Skyloom and Seed Innovations gives us approximately $259M without SkyWater - slightly above the top-end of their guidance of $245M, and in-line with my expectation that IonQ tend to set guidance low just to have it beat. Hitting this number would give them another year of 100% growth, continuing a powerful historic trend.

Now let's include SkyWater.

SkyWater was going through significant business transformation itself prior to the deal, having acquired Infineon's US Texas fab last year. Infineon is a German specialist semiconductor manufacturer who already has a prior relationship with IonQ, as they are the supplier of quantum circuits for Oxford Ionics, another IonQ company.

Post-transformation, SkyWater did revenue of $171M in Q4 2025 which translates to $684M annual run-rate.

SkyWater's 2026 revenue contribution to IonQ will largely depend on timing of the acquisition's completion - about $85.5M if it completes by Q2, or $43M if it completes by Q3, conservatively assume 0 growth.

Combined, I estimate IonQ will reach revenue of $302-345M in 2026, depending on timing of SkyWater acquisition.

In 2027, IonQ will have the full year revenue impact from all of its acquisitions. Without any growth at all, IonQ can simply "cruise" to a revenue of $945M ($234M baseline, $27M between Skyloom and Seed, and and $684M from SkyWater) - very close to a billion, 4 years ahead of ex-CEO Peter Chapman's 2030 target.

If we assume a continuation of 80% growth in IonQ's quantum business and 0 growth in the semiconductor fabrication business, IonQ's 2027 revenue will hit $1.15B. I expect actual results to come in somewhere between these 2 extremes.

Scenarios
Rev Projections Without SKYT Low-end with SKYT Upper-end with SKYT
2026 ($M) 259 302 345
2027 ($M) 259-466 945 1150

The SkyWater acquisition is structured as a cash-and-stock deal where each share of SKYT will be bought out with $15 of cash and 0.5265 IonQ shares (assuming IONQ stays below the collar floor of $37.99). With 48.6M shares of SKYT outstanding, this amounts to $729M cash and 25.6M shares of IONQ.

IonQ will also be assuming SkyWater's net debt of approximately 345M.

IONQ share count has risen to 366.6M after issuing 5M shares for Skyloom and Seed. SkyWater will further dilute the share count to 392.2M, which would give IonQ a market cap of close to $13B at current market price of around 33.

IonQ's net cash hoard is expected to decrease to $2.1B after SkyWater and 2 quarters of cash burn - reducing runway to 7 years, which is still ample.

Of course, we will have to re-evaluate everything if IonQ announces any further material acquisitions, but CEO Niccolo de Masi indicated a pause during a recent interview with Bloomberg, so perhaps we're entering a period of digestion and consolidation:

We've done a lot [of acquisitions] in 2025, and 2026 will be mostly powered by organic growth.

Long-term Projection (5 Years)

It's extremely challenging to project the long-term economics of any company going through rapid growth in an industry as dynamic as quantum. Nonetheless, we must attempt it as investors.

For this exercise, I'll try to answer the question of profitability - both when and how much. For that, I'll need to bring in a few more assumptions.

At the earnings call we learned that "remaining performance obligation" accelerated from $77M from end of 2024 to $370 at end of 2025. Intuitively, the acceleration makes sense as quantum computing is just starting to become commercially viable.

In fact, I expect a further "hockey-stick" acceleration in 2028/2029 as IonQ's quantum computers reach utility scale a full 2-3 years ahead of competitors, with >1000 logical qubits in production, and tape-out of their 200,000 physical qubit/8000 logical qubit system.

I'm baking in 100% growth in 2028 and 2029 before it starts tapering back down to 80% in 2030. For SkyWater, I'm conservatively assuming 0 growth, and I expect the that part of the business to weigh down overall growth rate of IonQ somewhat.

IonQ's gross margin was 40% in 2025, and SkyWater was 20% for the last 2 years. I expect these margins to be maintained.

IonQ has 686M of fixed costs excluding any cash interest income. SkyWater is already a roughly breakeven business as a standalone. It has 102M of annualized fixed costs post transformation.

IonQ may extinguish SkyWater debt with its warchest of cash, but if not, the interest income more than offsets any interests paid on the existing SkyWater debt. I will conservatively disregard the excess interests from the projection.

For 2026 & 2027 revenue numbers, I relied on previous section's analysis.

Putting it all together:

2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
IonQ Revenue 259 466 932 1,864 3,355
SKYT Revenue Contribution 324 684 684 684 684
Combined Revenue 583 1,150 1,616 2,548 4,039
Blended Growth % 97% 41% 58% 59%
Gross Margin 168 323 510 882 1,479
Fixed Costs 737 788 808 828 849
Pre-tax Earnings (569) (465) (298) 55 630

With the above assumptions, I expect IonQ to hit profitability by 2029. At the market cap of $13B, it is trading at 20.6x 2030 pre-tax earnings, which I feel is reasonable - even undervalued - for an industry leader going through transformative growth.

Risks

Needless to say, IonQ's valuation depends on it maintaining an outsized growth rate as an industry leader. And to do that, they must deliver on their ambitious technical roadmap.

They are set to deliver 256-qubit system based on the Oxford Ionics architecture this year. Investors should watch out for any indication of a delay - any major unexpected technical setback could make the rest of the roadmap questionable.

Another major risk factor is if IonQ makes any bad acquisitions that are material to the company. The acquisitions made so far mostly have good strategic fit, in my opinion, and the benefits outweighed the cost of dilution. However, the effects from one disastrous acquisition could take years to undo.

Takeaway

I was very accurate with my estimation of IonQ's 2025 results. In this article, I attempted to repeat that exercise for 2026, 2027 and beyond.

IonQ has consistently demonstrated high organic growth, and they have a habit of lowballing guidance and then beating the top-end.

Based on what is known about their guidance, latest acquisitions, and business prospects, I expect IonQ to reach over a billion in revenue by 2027, profitability by 2029, and become comfortably profitable by 2030.

Current valuation is very reasonable for investors who understand the potential of quantum computing, believe in IonQ's roadmap, and would like to take part in a public company going through startup-like hypergrowth.

data.title: What Happens After The S&P 500 Breaks Its 200-Day Moving Average
data.description:
While the war in the Middle East continues unabated, a new war of words broke out this week between President Trump and the Iranian officials with whom he says he is negotiating. This war is over markets. Trump wants lower oil prices (CL1:COM) and higher stock prices (SPY) for obvious reasons. Iran wants higher oil prices and lower stock prices to exact a punishing economic toll on the President in hopes that it will force him to retreat on their terms. Trump wants the conflict to end quickly, which he now defines as four to six weeks, while Iran wants to stretch it out as long as possible. The longer it lasts, the more economic pain for the U.S. economy.

This is why early Monday morning the President posted on his social media account that good and productive conversations with the Iranians were ongoing regarding ending the conflict. Whether that was true or not, stock prices soared and oil plunged more than $10, which was clearly the immediate objective. The Iranians countered in this war of words by denying any negotiations had occurred. Oil edged higher, and stocks gave back some of the gains the following day. Optimism in markets returned yesterday after news that the President’s 15-point plan to end the war was presented to Iranian officials through a Pakistani intermediary.

Yesterday’s optimism is waning again, with stock futures lower and oil higher, as the President’s proposal was dismissed and countered with five conditions that need to be met before Iran will agree to end the war. These include payment for war damages, mechanisms to ensure future attacks do not happen, a halt to all U.S. military operations, including in Israel and other Arab nations, and recognition of Iran’s sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz. The good news is that these proposals do constitute a form of negotiation. The bad news is that neither side is likely to get the entirety of their wish list, and a resolution looks highly unlikely by the end of this week, which is the President’s extended deadline from the original 48-hours he gave Iran on Saturday evening.

The President’s jawboning has helped limit the downside in risk assets, which I’ll call the Trump put, because investors know that all it takes is a post to send stocks soaring and oil prices plunging. He can do it at any time. The problem is that this can be used only so many times before investors start to question the validity of the claims. This tactic may work a time or two more, but if the Iranians continue to assert false claims, then it will start to fall on deaf investors ears.

From an investment standpoint, the key variable here is time. The longer it takes to reach an agreement that ends the conflict and reopens the strait, the more damage that occurs to the economy, increasing the probability of an economic downturn later this year. This gives Iran leverage in the negotiations, as they continued to launch missile and drone strikes on Wednesday, despite assertions that their military capabilities have been wiped out.

Meanwhile, gasoline prices have risen $1 over the past month to a national average of $4/gallon, diesel is up $1.60 to $5.37/gallon, and the selloff in Treasuries over deficit and inflation concerns has increased interest rates, driving mortgage rates from just under to well over 6%. Costs for materials and other inputs are rising for businesses at the fastest pace since the energy spike that occurred when Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022. The rate of inflation will rise in coming months, and the rate of economic growth will slow in a spat of stagflation that this President can’t afford in advance of a heated midterm election. This is the war that Iran is starting to win, and it has nothing to do with the battlefield.

This is why I think the President will make haste in negotiations with another optimistic announcement about negotiations on Friday, which will be followed by news that he has struck “a great deal” for all parties some time the following week. The details won’t matter if it ends the bombing, reopens the strait, and compels Israel to comply with the President’s demands. The long-term damage to the economy should be minimal, while I think investors will look past the short-term pain as temporary. Pundits more pessimistic than me point to the fact that the S&P 500 broke its long-term uptrend during this conflict on March 19, which indicates this could be the beginning of a bear market. The odds are not in their favor.

The S&P 500 has broken its 200-day moving average 153 times since 1957, and the returns that followed were not ominous at all. While the market’s performance in the month of the break is mixed, the index was higher 73% of the time three months later for an average gain of 3.5%. The win rate improves to 87% for a gain of 9.8% over the following six months. Investors saw gains 80% of the time in the 12 months that followed, averaging a return of 15.9%. A key distinction between the wins and losses was the length of time we remained below the 200-day. Short-term breaks were far more bullish, which is why the clock is ticking for President Trump in his war of words with Iran.

Volatility in markets always creates opportunities, and the one that stands out to me now is in precious metals. Gold and silver have sold off along with other risk assets, unlike what one would expect during times of geopolitical uncertainty. I attribute this to the significant outperformance for both metals since last November, which was fueled largely by new investors who viewed both metals more like meme stocks than portfolio hedges. I suspect this crowd has been washed out with bear market declines in this space over the past month, creating deeply oversold conditions.

Gold (GLD) successfully tested its 200-day moving average last week, preserving its uptrend. The same can be said for silver (SLV). These are good entry points for new or additional investment dollars.
